title: Art History Survey I
description: This art history course is a chronological overview of artworks from the prehistoric period up to the 15th-century Renaissance period. This course introduces students to the major historical monuments of world art and various art forms from different cultures and periods. The course explores how individual artworks express form, style, and cultural meaning while introducing students to art historical vocabulary and various art historical research methods. The class meets twice a week and features a mix of lectures and student participation.

title: Art History Survey II
description: The second semester of the art history survey course combines a chronological overview of art works from the seventeenth century through up to 1960 with a rigorous investigation of the contexts in which art has been made. The course covers major art historical developments in the western and non-western worlds with an evaluation of their form, content, style, cultural meaning, and historical import. The course also introduces students to art historical vocabulary and various methods of art historical research. Each section meets twice a week for lecture and student discussion.

title: Kill Yr Semiotics: A Critical History of Post-Punk
description: Kill Yr Semiotics is a seminar devoted to a cultural history of the experimental lyricism of post-punk through the analysis of music, film, poetry, prose, art, and theory with a particular emphasis on 1970s’ New York and postmodernism. An expanded spatial/temporal dialogue regarding a range of artists such as Lorna Simpson, Valie Export, Robert Smithson, Joan Jonas, Robert Mapplethorpe, Richard Serra, Patti Smith, Gordon Matta-Clark, Yvonne Rainer, Ellen Gallagher, Eileen Myles, Stan Brakhage, The Talking Heads, and Television will challenge the legacy of the post-punk as an anti-aesthetic. Kill Yr Semiotics will revisit the clichés of punk as well as look at such proto-punk precedents as Arthur Rimbaud, The Velvet Underground, Karl Marx, Yoko Ono, Fluxus, and The Situationists in order to triangulate the expressive and iconic role of history, race, class, gender, religion, and urbanism in the post-punk arts.

title: Cultural Remembrance & Traditions of Commemoration
description: From traditions of oral storytelling to retell the tales of ancestors to constructing monuments and memorials to people, battles, and events to parades and rituals, humans have always had the impulse to remember the past and to construct narratives. What do we commemorate and why?  Who gets to decide how a society and culture remember past events? What happens when commonly understood historical narratives change? In this course, we will study the history, theories, and forms of public memory to try and understand this basic human impulse and answer these questions. We will learn theories and terms for different types of remembrance (social, collective, public) and look at frameworks for how societies remember. We will examine histories of storytelling, monuments, memorials, landmarks, and museums, from ancient Greece to the present. We will explore how individuals, organizations, and governments are instrumental to what becomes of the commemorative landscape, and we will look at what happens after statues are taken down due to reckoning with the past. We will consider the role of artists in the work of remembering the past, and explore the many different shapes and forms commemoration can take.

title: (W) Feminist Art Practices in the 20th & 21st Century
description: Feminist art cannot be understood as a style, like Art Nouveau or Expressionism. Neither can it be simply defined as artworks created by women. Feminist art practices express criticism of cultural, structural and ideological inequalities with regard to gender, – 2 – ACADEMIC STUDIES   [continued] sexuality, race, class, and nationality, while proposing alternative, experimental solutions. In other words, feminist art practices constitute a field of inquiry that is both socially and aesthetically driven; entanglements of form and ideology, representation and politics are questioned, deconstructed, and remade. Although this course primarily focuses on the development of feminist art practices in Western Culture, we will expand our inquiry to the global level, considering how categories of gender and sexuality are constructed differently across cultures and redressed by other feminist art practices. Through weekly readings, class discussions, written assignments, and oral presentations, students will consider how gender is relevant to the creation and study of art and culture. Important to this course are the relationships between art and critical theory, including feminism, Marxism, psychoanalysis, semiotics, post-modernism, ecofeminism, and other theories of race and gender. Please note that this is a WRITING INTENSIVE course, which means you will be expected to complete numerous writing and research assignments.

title: (S) Indigeneity, Objects, and Exhibitionism
description: The relation between Indigeneity and objecthood is complex. For example, the colonial museum plundered Indigenous art and communities as part of its global project; the Indigenous body itself was put on display, exhibited as an “exotic” object for European consumption. Yet, Indigenous makers have always long preceded colonial contact. In this class, we will focus on Indigenous fine art and craft in order to think about a decolonial approach to objects. In particular, we will think about performance, or exhibitionism, as a way of reworking the colonial contours of the body as a site of practice.

title: (W) Experiments: Artistic Research
description: How can research shape studio practice? How does an artist use research to propel the creative process? Experiments in Artistic Research will focus on beginning and developing a major student-driven independent research project that connects liberal studies with the studio. The course will be divided into three concurrent parts. In the first part, students will be introduced to the field of artistic or creative approaches to research. Students will study key examples of artistic/creative research as well as debates about the role of research in the artistic/creative process. The second part of the course will concentrate on building practical research skills. How, for example, do we ask questions that generate useful or usable information? How do we begin a research process? How does our research evolve as we get further into the material? How does research relate to artistic practice? Finally, we will create a significant textual or multimedia work as an outgrowth of the research processes we have learned. The format that this work takes (essay, narrative or creative non-fiction, visual essay, etc.) will be determined by the student in collaboration with the professor.

title: (W) Art History Minor Thesis
description: Students pursuing the minor may enroll in this course either semester. Students work with a thesis advisor. Independent Study: 3 credits/semester; 3 hours/week.

title: Millennium Approaches: America in the 21st Century
description: *Requirement for all Sophomores. Course not available for Junior or Senior enrollment. The class is organized around five phrases that have become part of contemporary popular discourse, and each phrase is tied to key moments or events in 21st Century America. Using a diverse range of media—from political speeches to film and from memoir to music videos—and indexing key humanities concepts, the class traces the “reverb” of the event, helping students understand the historical context of contemporary conditions. Our key moments are five: The 2025 Executive Orders; the 2008 Obama election; the George Floyd uprising of 2020; the – 3 – ACADEMIC STUDIES   [continued] establishment of Guantanamo Bay in a post-9/11 world; and the anti-immigration rhetoric of 2016. Taking these as our points of departure we turn back and trace these moments through slavery, European migration into the United States, Japanese internment, the AIDS crisis, as well as immigration and the southern border. Requirement for all Sophomores: 3 credits/semester; 3 hours/week.

title: (W) Creative Writing
description: While visual artists work with material like paint, paper, pigment, stone, metal, fabric, and ink, the writer’s primary material is language. In this class, we will explore language as a creative medium with close attention to the ways in which language already informs – or can inform –  artistic practice. Seeking both to enhance established modes and develop new possibilities, we will consider the ways in which writing can help us cultivate receptivity to inspiration, forge connections to other artists, and fruitfully revise our work. In this class, you will be invited to experiment with creative writing as an art form and a medium of communication, reflection, and revision that can embolden and animate your visual practice.

title: Slow Reading: The Joy of the Word & Mindful Concept-Making
description: This course has one simple objective: stopping. An attempt at creating a refuge from the need for ceaseless production, uninterrupted statement making, and incessant certainty. We will read four short novellas — giving ourselves one month to stay and live with each story and their worlds. Instead of rushing to understand and determine ‘the Truth of things’ , we will speculate, ponder, and slow down our individual and collective thought processes. During the semester, students will consider questions pertaining to historical processes and their ongoing aftermaths, metaphysical and philosophical issues, and imaging and living otherwise (among other speculations). The main goals will be to create critical and creative writing works that strive towards a political economy of care and collectivity.

title: (S) ReWorlding: Environmental Humanities Approaches to Climate Crisis
description: We live in the Anthropocene, an epoch in which human activity has fundamentally altered the surface of the planet, from its oceans to its landmasses to its atmosphere. In order to respond in a way that makes any sense whatsoever, we must untangle the human histories of resource extraction and consumption that have long imperiled Life on spaceship Earth. In this course, we will explore art, literature, music, and film that grapple with the root causes of climate change and that envision new forms of human and planetary coexistence.

title: Global Poetry
description: This course will (fail to) introduce poetry in its infinity. From the Xuanji Tu (         ) - the "Star Gauge" or "Map of the Armillary Sphere"–, a 4th century Chinese writing that produces over 4,000 different poems from a single poem to Chrystos manifesto-like and erotics-filled poetry, the class will bring the students to the generative possibilities of poetic discourse. During the semester, students will read a multitude of poets from all over the globe by engaging with different genres and historical periods to produce their own poetry collections.

title: Body Politic: Gender, Sexuality, and State Power
description: While bodies may seem at some levels to be autonomous and distinct, many important philosophers and theorists have explored the ways in which gender and sexual identity condition access to freedom and agency. In this course, we will encounter a range of essays – 4 – ACADEMIC STUDIES   [continued] written by feminist thinkers. Though it will not aim to be comprehensive, the syllabus will consider writers from the Enlightenment to the twenty-first century and will pay special attention to innovations in racial feminisms and queer theory.

title: (W) Writing Minor Capstone
description: Writing Capstone is the capstone course of the Writing Minor. Each student will focus on an individual writing project totaling at least 100 pages: a novella; memoir; a play or series of plays; compilation of poetry; short story collection; compilation of essays/blog entries; graphic novel, etc. This individual writing project will be “workshopped” in a writing group of peer editors--fellow Writing Workshop class members--led by the professor.  Multiple drafts of each project will be required in response to the group’s feedback. The culmination of the course will be the publication of each student’s work, either through self-publishing or traditional publishing.

title: (S) Sustainable Ecosystems: Art & Design Minor Capstone
description: The SEAD Capstone is designed to give students an opportunity to draw upon all of the Academic threads of the minor--art history, humanities, philosophy, and science--and develop a final research project that culminates in a studio project relevant to their SEAD studies. Each student will be expected to integrate environmental justice and ethics, sustainability, and design to produce a cohesive and practical arts-based project that addresses a local environmental issue. Through this project, students will demonstrate planning, communication, and leadership skills as well as a substantial studio effort. The projects may include a proposed design solution, an individual work or activist project made in response to a specific local climate concern or issue, etc. A statement of purpose and a project plan is required; the project concept and scope must be approved by the Coordinator of the SEAD Minor.

title: (PE) (S) Natural History-Coastal Ecosystems
description: This course provides students with an opportunity to explore and study the flora and fauna inhabiting local intertidal zones such as tidal marsh, beach/dune, and rocky shore. Attention is on the basic principles of ecology and the identification of more common marine life forms. Laboratory and field studies provide a means of studying the many interesting adaptations associated with intertidal organisms. Two field trips are required.

title: (S) Sustainable Agriculture
description: Sustainable Agriculture is a nuanced and dynamic field shaped by the interplay of ecological, cultural, economic, and social systems. Agriculture encompasses diverse landscapes, practices, and motivations, each influenced by access to resources and the broader contexts in which farming takes place. This course examines how food production can adapt to the challenges of climate change, shifting resources, and increasing demand while sustaining our cultural and personal connections to land and food. Through engagement with community farmers, agricultural educators, and the text Farming While Black by Leah Penniman, students investigate the principles of sustainable and regenerative agriculture. Together we will critically examine how equity, ecology, and tradition intersect in farming, and envision farm futures that balance resilience, justice, and cultural connection.x

title: Intro to Biodesign
description: Intro to Biodesign explores how art and design meaningfully engages living systems, ecological intelligence, systems and emerging biotechnologies. This course centers on developing scientific literacy within creative practice. Students learn how to read scientific publication, interpret data, evaluate sources, and translate research into conceptual and material inquiry. Through research, discussion, experimentation, and short creative studies, we examine Life’s Principles, bioethics, speculative design, green technology, new materials and the implications of shaping biofutures.  This course builds fluency in interdisciplinary dialogue and equips students with the research frameworks necessary to responsibly incorporate living systems into their work. No prior science background required.

title: (AE) Money Matters
description: Money Matters will cover topics of personal finance. We will explore the fundamentals of how to earn money, manage it, spend wisely, save and invest. Topics include budgeting, credit, savings, taxes, loans, investments, employment benefits and financial goals.

title: Introduction to Marxist Theory
description: Karl Marx reimagined philosophy, seeing it less as a mode of abstract speculation and more as a crucial component in a larger project of changing the world.  In order to support this project, Marx developed a constellation of critical concepts meant to shed light on the modern world, including alienation, exploitation, commodity fetishism, ideology, contradiction, and historical materialism to name only a few.  The history of Marxist theory can be read as the attempt to elaborate, reconstruct and augment these critical concepts in changing historical conditions.  This course introduces Marx’s philosophical work and more recent innovations in Marxist theory in order to consider whether the Marxist critical tradition helps us to better understand our current situation.

title: African American History
description: It was a hundred years ago—1926—that famed African American scholar Carter G. Woodson established "Negro History Week" in an effort to cement Black history as part and parcel of American history. To know the history of the race, Woodson would have argued, was to know not only the sons and daughters of Africa, but also, and as crucially, the pitfalls, possibilities, and promises of these United States. So contended Woodson, so contended the African American historians who followed him, and so contends this course, one which begins on the shores of Africa and narrates up to present day. And while this history of the US, as it must, deals in enslavement and execution, defilement and the draconian, it also—as it must—tells of visionaries and renaissance, movements and change. It is a history whose complexity nourished the complex and oft-contradictory nation which we all inhabit.

title: Introduction to Drawing
description: This course introduces drawing from perception, including techniques for measurement, contour, massing, volume, and rendering illumination through value. Compositional skills such as placement and figure/ground interaction are stressed.  Drawing materials include vine charcoal, compressed charcoal, stamp, and india ink; subject matter includes still life, interior, landscape, and the figure.

title: Color & Drawing: Mixed Media
description: This course represents the convergence of two lines of inquiry: color theory and drawing. It is for those students interested in deepening their experience with color as a formal dynamic and as a carrier of meaning. Material and conceptual exploration is facilitated through the flexible, serial processes of drawing. Students use drawing practice as a means to focus their attention on how color can be used, making many images and taking risks that may in turn inform the more time- and materially-intensive work of other studio disciplines.

title: Advanced Drawing- (Capstone students only)
description: This course concentrates on creating a structured environment in which students can begin to build independent working processes and linear bodies of work. It is designed to complement the experience in the major studio, despite the student's chosen area of concentration. Emphasis is placed on developing content driven work and research skills, while continuing to focus on the formal aspects of a drawing experience. Course serves as the capstone experience for the Drawing Minor.

title: Digital Imaging & Fabrication
description: This section explores the fundamentals of digital photography. Emphasis is placed on developing a proficiency with Adobe Photoshop, cameras, and printing in the pursuit of Foundation design and composition objectives.

title: 3D Materiality: Sculpture. week 1-7
description: This course investigates three-dimensional phenomena and cultivates an understanding of design and composition in three dimensions. This course equips students with the ability to create and discuss three-dimensional work with intention and precision. Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

This course equips students with the ability to create and discuss three-dimensional work with intention and precision. Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: Two-Dimensional Design
description: This course cultivates an understanding of design and composition through an in-depth investigation of two-dimensional phenomena and perceptual color. Through the successful completion of carefully sequenced exercises, students will gain an understanding of composition and design while developing the dexterity and skills with digital tools necessary to create work with intention and precision. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: Intro to Game Development
description: This course will introduce students to the foundational aspects of game development.  Starting with elemental lessons that teach students the basics of working in game engines and give insights about the game industry on a whole, we’ll move into design- or theory-based projects about game mechanics and art production. Game development is a complex, multidisciplinary practice, and this course will serve as the bedrock for the rest of your gamedev journey here at MECA&D.

title: Animation I
description: This course introduces and explores the twelve principles of animation as developed at the Disney Animation Studios. Assigned projects guide students through the technical and creative processes involved in 2D digital animation. Students apply the principles to create sequential drawings that give personality and emotion to moving objects and simple characters.

title: Storyboarding for Animation
description: In animation, storytelling is about much more than writing a script. This course will explore the full story development pipeline from loglines and storybeats to finished animatic. We will cover the basics of narrative structure, cinematic grammar, and story-based character development with a specific focus on developing narrative or experimental work for animation.

title: Character Animation
description: Key poses, gestures, and timing are just a few of the principles that animators use to breathe life into characters. Animators also apply subtle or overt acting cues to convey thought and emotion through their characters. In this class, we deeply explore personality and emotion as students generate compelling performances in their medium of choice (2D, 3D, Stop-motion).

title: Interfaces for Games
notes:
GD xlist- please avoid conflict w/ GD majors
New Course Proposal approved Fall25
description: In this course we will focus on creating Graphical User Interfaces (GUI) for games. Through case studies we will explore the use of visual hierarchies, color, typography, symbol systems, and nomenclature to effectively communicate with players and guide their actions in game. Students will apply knowledge of visual design principles and the process of iterative design to create wireframes, prototypes, and high-fidelity mockups. Assignments will challenge students to utilize design fundamentals to present game information in ways that are both visually engaging and easily understood. The assignments in this course are game-related, but the lessons are broadly applicable to the wider world of digital interfaces.

title: Intro to 3D Modeling & Animation
description: Great characters don’t happen by accident—they are the result of deliberate, thoughtful design. In this course, we will analyze iconic characters from games and animation, breaking down what makes them memorable and effective. Through a progressive, iterative process, students will develop their own characters with a focus on visual impact, audience relevance, and personality-driven design. We will emphasize structured iteration, exploring how shape, proportion, silhouette, and detail work together to strengthen a character’s identity. Additionally, students will refine their designs through backstory development, prop exploration, and stylization. By the end of the course, students will have fully realized character concepts ready for entertainment design.

title: Background Design for Animation
description: "Background Design" offers an in-depth exploration of the pivotal role background design plays in the visual narrative of animated storytelling. This course is crafted for students passionate about the intricate art of creating immersive worlds and environments within the realm of animation. The coursework will encompass practical applications. Students will learn to create backgrounds using various techniques and styles while utilizing industry-standard software and pipelines. Students will also advance their understanding of perspective and their ability to visualize complex 3D environments and camera movements in 2D compositions. Strong emphasis will be placed on iteration and exploration of design languages that will challenge the students to move beyond their current understandings of design principles. Students will learn how those principles are applied to the backgrounds of animated works to build visual narratives, enhance mood, and underscore emotional resonance. By the conclusion of the course, students will have honed their skills in creating visually compelling and contextually relevant backgrounds for animation.

title: Stop-Motion and FX
description: This course will cover a range of tools and techniques used to create stop-motion animation. Through these processes we will explore the relationships between animation, sound and visual effects. Students will learn to use D-SLR cameras and stage lights in tandem with professional stop-motion software. They will also learn to build and animate both 2-dimensional and 3-dimensional puppets.

title: Interactive & Immersive Art: Experience Design and Installation
notes:
Crosslist with any department interested
New Course Proposal approved Fall25
description: Interactive & Immersive Art: Experience Design and Installation is a multidiscipline course focused on the collaborative design and production of an immersive experiential installation augmented with interactive and technology elements. Students will learn how to work and communicate outside of their discipline with an emphasis on audience experience. The class will explore how immersion and interaction can be used to develop a narrative in a larger collaborative piece of art. We will experiment and iterate on what the – 12 – STUDIO & SEMINAR COURSES   [continued] audience's experience will be. The class will work towards a common vision and develop project management skills and experience dealing with a complex install that juggles different types of art and technology all coming together to create something new. Some examples of technology we will explore include projection mapping and using cameras and sensor data to interact with visual and audio elements in the exhibit. Students' individual use of technology is optional based on interest and ultimate design of the piece. Students from ALL areas of the college are encouraged to take this course. The more varied set of skills we start with the better.

title: Major Production Studio I
description: Students develop and create work specific to their area of interest, building their visual and verbal vocabularies around the making and critiquing of animation and/or game projects or assets. Faculty are available for mentoring on technical and project management support. Three projects are finished and presented in group critiques in this class. Importance is placed on extending and mastering skills, developing ideas, and production scheduling and management. All projects will result in finished work for inclusion in a portfolio to be developed in SEM 451. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Major Production Studio I
description: Students develop and create work specific to their area of interest, building their visual and verbal vocabularies around the making and critiquing of animation and/or game projects or assets. Faculty are available for mentoring on technical and project management support. Three projects are finished and presented in group critiques in this class. Importance is placed on extending and mastering skills, developing ideas, and production scheduling and management. All projects will result in finished work for inclusion in a portfolio to be developed in SEM 451. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Special Topics: (AE) Social Media Entrepreneurship
description: This course explores the emerging opportunities for independent animators, artists, and creators in the digital landscape. Platforms like TikTok, Instagram Reels, and YouTube allow individuals to build audiences, establish their brands, and generate income as solopreneurs. Students will analyze the successes of independent artists, learn practical strategies for content creation, and develop their unique online presence. By the end of the course, students will have a clear roadmap to building a sustainable online brand and monetizing their creative work.

title: Special Topics: Shaders & VFX
description: From a crackling campfire in the dark, to the sparkle that bursts when you collect a powerup, to goopy green acid that bubbles around a villain’s lair, visual effects (VFX) are a crucial vehicle for delivering information about mood, threats, goals, and rewards to a player.  This course covers not only the tools used to author particle effects and shaders, but also the high level philosophies that drive the design process of such assets.  Part animation, part tech art, part painting, but explosively enjoyable throughout.

title: Major Capstone Project Pre-Production
description: Students develop a project that will be completed in AG 422 Major Capstone Production. Pre-production will include scripts, project planning, character development, audio production, storyboards, animatics, environmental art, production schedules, etc. Faculty mentorship and group critiques will support project development. All projects will result in process assets for inclusion in the portfolio developed in SEM 451 Professional Studio. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Major Capstone Project Pre-Production
description: Students develop a project that will be completed in AG 422 Major Capstone Production. Pre-production will include scripts, project planning, character development, audio production, storyboards, animatics, environmental art, production schedules, etc. Faculty mentorship and group critiques will support project development. All projects will result in process assets for inclusion in the portfolio developed in SEM 451 Professional Studio. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: (M) Elements of Music I
description: With a focus on listening, creating and performing music, students will learn the basics of music literacy including rhythm, pitch, timbre, harmony, and form.  Examples of music from various cultures and time periods will provide a broad context for understanding music notation in its many different forms.  Students with no previous music experience will finish with a working ability to read and write music using standard notation. Connections between visual and auditory thinking will be emphasized.

title: Instrumental Lab: Guitar  (1 cr. class/ week 1-7)
description: Whether you’re a beginner or intermediate level guitar player, this 7-week group lesson course will help you get to the next level of your guitar playing knowledge and ability. Students are expected to supply their own instruments but there are a few available for those without. If you don’t have a guitar, please contact the instructor before class begins.

title: Instrumental Lab: Percussion (1 cr. class/ week 1-7)
description: If you are already an experienced percussionist/drummer or a beginner, this 7-week group lesson course is designed to increase your skills to play and understand rhythm.

title: Sound Design – Painting in the Sonic Realm
description: In this course, students will explore and engage in the world of sound design, using ambient, improvisational, traditional, and experimental soundmaking to create sonic environments. Emphasizing a hands-on approach and experimentation, students will be introduced to various techniques and methods to provoke mood and emotion with both musical and non-musical instruments and objects. Field recording, Foley, synthesis, sampling, looping, and multi-tracking will be learned through a hybrid of analog and digital hardware and software to focus on building sonic space by layering music and sound. In addition to a brief survey of ambient and experimental music, students will apply skills used in this course to support film, animation, podcasts, live performance or site- specific sound installations.

title: (M) Music Minor Capstone
description: This course is a culminating experience for seniors in the minor.  Students will build upon the skills and concepts introduced in the Music Minor to realize a project of substantial effort within a semester. Examples include: scoring a film/animation, producing a live performance, recording a 6-Song EP of original work, creating an installation that incorporates sound, or writing a 10-song collection. A statement of purpose and a project plan is required. Students work with a faculty advisor under an independent study arrangement. The faculty advisor and student meet prior, during and at the completion of the capstone project. Through this project, students will demonstrate their skills and understanding of how music and sound both affects and supports their art or how they have further developed within the field of sound/music. The concept and scope of the project must be approved by the Coordinator of the Bob Crewe Program in Art and Music.

title: Ceramics Handbuilding
description: Class assignments emphasize the activity of making objects and the development of strong forms in clay. Students are introduced to essential hand-building construction methods. Class assignments reflect the diversity within the ceramic medium, and thus, students will be exposed to a range of functional and sculptural issues. Red earthenware is the primary clay body used in class assignments. Students will learn low-fire surfacing techniques, including terra sigillata, colored slip, underglaze, and glaze. The Raku process will also be covered. Examples of historical and contemporary ceramic work will be shown to provide context and suggest possibilities.

title: Ceramics Throwing- Beginning
description: Wheel throwing is the primary means of making ceramic forms in this course. Introductory and intermediate throwing skills, material concepts, glazing, and firing are covered. Reduction kiln firing concepts are introduced as the students construct functional and nonfunctional forms in clay. Students also gain familiarity with artists who use wheel processes to explore contemporary issues and design. The Raku process will also be covered.

title: (AE) From Clay to Commerce
description: This course is designed to equip students with the essential skills and knowledge required to develop, ideate, and successfully bring a product to market. Through a combination of theoretical concepts, practical exercises, and real-world case studies, students will gain a comprehensive understanding of the product development process, from conception to production to market launch.

title: Glaze Chemistry & Kiln Firing
description: This course exposes students to ceramic materiality and surface possibilities at various temperatures. A basic understanding of the chemistry of glaze formulation through lectures, demonstrations, and hands-on experience leads students to experimentation and testing for different firing ranges, color, and texture possibilities. This hands-on Lab exploration enhances students' ability to develop a personal vocabulary of materiality and process for use in studio work. Knowledge of ceramic materials will also include safe handling and use while training students to fire kilns under department supervision safely.

title: Ceramics III: Major Studio. [anticipate 6 juniors]
description: This course is the major studio component for the fall semester of the junior year in the ceramics major. The course fosters a personal sense of direction, initially through structured assignments that ultimately lead to self-directed work. During the semester, students increase their vocabulary of skill and concepts. Students also learn to relate their work to historical and contemporary issues. Additionally, students learn the operation of various kilns and explore various clay bodies that are appropriate for their work. A combination of group discussions and demonstrations, shared and individual crits, and one-on-one dialogue further augment the growth and maturation of studio work. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Ceramics IV: Major Studio [anticipate 2 seniors]
description: This course is the primary studio component of the fall semester of the senior year in the ceramics major. It is designed to continue to foster the development of a personal sense of direction. Students continue to relate their work to historical and contemporary issues in the medium. During the fall semester, self-directed studio assignments, topical discussions, and readings from periodicals and books expose contemporary issues and encourage the students to develop a collection of personal source material. Visiting artists’ presentations and trips to galleries and museums augment classroom activities. Individual and group critiques promote opportunities for the thoughtful articulation of ideas and feedback from instructors and peers. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Typography I
description: This course introduces the fundamentals of typography as a vehicle of expression and communication — skills that are valuable to virtually all art and design disciplines. Students will learn to identify and classify type by looking closely at the anatomy of letterforms and will gain an understanding of the historical contexts, tools, and methods that have produced various styles of type. Projects will employ type to convey informational hierarchy, integrate type with images effectively, and experiment with Gestalt principles to create visually compelling compositions. Graphic Design Major requirement. 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week. No prerequisite.

title: Special Topics: Motion Design
description: Explore movement as a powerful form of visual communication. Projects and exercises will guide students through the 12 principles of animation, 2D, 2.5D, and 3D animation methods and workflows, and an introduction to audio integration. Detailed demos and lectures will introduce topics including timing and spacing, sequence, modularity and loops, parabolas and pendulums, hierarchical motion, and cause and effect. Projects build on students’ knowledge of vector shape language, visual systems, and typography through the addition of motion. Open to all majors, the course covers the fundamentals of modern animation tools, including AfterEffects, Cavalry, Adobe Animate, and Autodesk Maya.

title: History of Graphic Design [anticipate 6 juniors]
description: Lectures, readings, discussions, student presentations, and studio projects will investigate design history and contemporary issues that shape how we perceive the practice of design. This serves to expand the individual student’s personal palette of form and context. Graphic Design Major requirement. 3 credits; 6 hours/week.

title: Senior Studio. [anticipate 8 seniors]
description: This project-based studio course emphasizes the potential for artistic/conceptual expression within the practice of the designer. Sequential and intensive projects will focus on the presenting, articulating, editing, and refining of work that defines individual designer portfolios. End results are more emphasized as well as evidence of a personal working methodology. This course often refers to professional situations, production methods, and application. Graphic Design Major requirement. 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Intro to Ink for Illustrators
description: The tradition of pen and ink has continued to thrive in the modern day and still is one of the most popular mediums for many illustrators and graphic artists. The use of inking is commonly used in the development of graphic novels, comics, and other various forms of illustration. Learned techniques can often be transferred to a digital medium. This class is an introduction to the fundamental and inventive use of drawing with pen, brush, and ink. Course projects cover a full repertoire of practice sheets, and line-making techniques; including cross-hatching, gesture, stippling, freestyle lines, experimentation with invented tools, as well as brushwork and ink washes. There will be a focus on materials and demonstrations explaining the proper use of pen, nibs, brushes, ink, and paper choices. Students will be exposed to a range of illustrators/graphic artists who work primarily with ink.

title: Intro to Ink for Illustrators
description: The tradition of pen and ink has continued to thrive in the modern day and still is one of the most popular mediums for many illustrators and graphic artists. The use of inking is commonly used in the development of graphic novels, comics, and other various forms of illustration. Learned techniques can often be transferred to a digital medium. This class is an introduction to the fundamental and inventive use of drawing with pen, brush, and ink. Course projects cover a full repertoire of practice sheets, and line-making techniques; including cross-hatching, gesture, stippling, freestyle lines, experimentation with invented tools, as well as brushwork and ink washes. There will be a focus on materials and demonstrations explaining the proper use of pen, nibs, brushes, ink, and paper choices. Students will be exposed to a range of illustrators/graphic artists who work primarily with ink.

title: Portrait Drawing in Illustration: People and Animals
description: This course is a beginning-level portraiture class and is devoted to the study of portrait work as it pertains to the field of illustration. Assignments will be based on portrait work in a range of styles from realistic and detailed to stripped-down and minimalist. Human forms will be studied extensively (and traditionally) within the first few weeks. Live models and photo references will be the basis for much of this study. Animal forms will be introduced in a similar fashion. Assignments will range from in-class studies to homework involving the execution of finished work with the portrait featured in an illustrative context.

title: Setting the Scene
description: Backgrounds should never be an afterthought or considered filler. Care and consideration in creating backgrounds should be as important as the characters who dwell in them. This class teaches the fundamentals of constructing spaces, environments, and settings, in themselves and tailored for characters." World-build with props, vehicles, architecture, and natural forms on par with character work. Focus on perspective drawing including linear one, two-point, and isometric, as well as exposure to alternative approaches, and constructive drawing will be used as systems to manipulate observed, referenced, and imagined sources alike, shoring up sound 2-D design principles to create effective compositions for illustrative ends.

title: Setting the Scene

This class teaches the fundamentals of constructing spaces, environments, and settings, in themselves and tailored for characters." World-build with props, vehicles, architecture, and natural forms on par with character work. Focus on perspective drawing including linear one, two-point, and isometric, as well as exposure to alternative approaches, and constructive drawing will be used as systems to manipulate observed, referenced, and imagined sources alike, shoring up sound 2-D design principles to create effective compositions for illustrative ends.

title: The Picture Book
description: This course introduces students to the picture book genre and illustrating for children. Students will study the picture book craft by reading from books on hand, dissecting their structure, and defining the visual elements that carry the story. Students will choose stories to illustrate and develop storyboards, book dummies, and at least three final illustrations. Through discovery and risk-taking, students will experiment with a variety of media and be exposed to topics ranging from composition, sequential drive, character design, narrative flow, integration of image and text, and drawing diversity. There will be studio work, guests, demonstrations, and regular critiques of work-in-progress, as well as exposure to professional practices within the publishing industry.

title: Digital Mixed Media for Illustrators
description: A digital studio course for students interested in exploring advanced illustration-based projects combining digital techniques with traditional methods to expand on introductory-level digital skills and develop new processes and approaches for creating unique illustrations.

title: Illustration Majors Studio. [anticipate 26 juniors]
description: This course further exposes students to a range of illustration formats and genres. Students hone drawing and technical skills, consider material choices, establish research methods, and explore concepts. Students work to solve visual problems with short and long deadlines incorporating stages of formal and conceptual refinement. A student may work in representational, narrative, or conceptual genres on assignment-based projects, and will gain experience in contemporary practices, including process, the articulation of ideas, and presentation. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Illustration Majors Studio
description: This course further exposes students to a range of illustration formats and genres. Students hone drawing and technical skills, consider material choices, establish research methods, and explore concepts. Students work to solve visual problems with short and long deadlines incorporating stages of formal and conceptual refinement. A student may work in representational, narrative, or conceptual genres on assignment-based projects, and will gain experience in contemporary practices, including process, the articulation of ideas, and presentation. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Illustration Majors Studio  [anticipate 18 seniors]
description: This course continues to build on strengths acquired from the previous three years of study/major. Individuality will be a focus. The trajectory of this class is to build a body of work that best reflects the highest of each student’s abilities. Students will be measured against themselves throughout the semester. There will be a strong emphasis on problem solving while continuing to develop one’s own voice and visual language through illustration. Concept, technique, design, work ethic, and professionalism will be stressed. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Illustration Majors Studio
description: This course continues to build on strengths acquired from the previous three years of study/major. Individuality will be a focus. The trajectory of this class is to build a body of work that best reflects the highest of each student’s abilities. Students will be measured against themselves throughout the semester. There will be a strong emphasis on problem solving while continuing to develop one’s own voice and visual language through illustration. Concept, technique, design, work ethic, and professionalism will be stressed. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Metalsmithing & Jewelry I
description: This one-semester introductory course blends technical instruction with an investigation of design and concept as it relates to ornament and function. Students develop confidence and proficiency with the basic skills of forming, soldering, and finishing. Classroom discussions and image presentations introduce the broad range of possibilities that the field of Metalsmithing and Jewelry encompasses that are part of this diverse and exciting field.

title: Designing - More than One
description: In this course students will become proficient with a number of fabrication and casting techniques that lend themselves to creating multiples, whether one focuses on developing a collection for limited production or investigates how repetition, pattern and rhythm can be developed by utilizing the same element again and again. Students will move through a design process that will consider material, modes of production/labor and their relationship to form and content. Mold making, casting, die forming, linkage and more will be covered.

title: Metalsmithing & Jewelry Majors Studio. [anticipate 6 juniors]
description: This one-year class introduces students to the full breadth of the studio practice of the Metalsmithing and Jewelry major. Students build on their previous skills while learning new technical skills. Students will develop a solid conceptual base for their work and discover modes of working and interests that are specific to them through research, drawing and model making as well as finished – 18 – STUDIO & SEMINAR COURSES   [continued] pieces. Incorporated into the curriculum are projects that challenge students both conceptually and technically. In addition to assignments, self-designed explorations of technical, conceptual or design directions are included. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Metalsmithing & Jewelry Majors Studio. [anticipate 2 seniors]
description: The final year provides students with the opportunity to work in an independent manner. New technical skills may be introduced, yet emphasis is placed on honing already acquired skills. Each student is expected to select, research, and articulate a direction that culminates into a related body of work and written thesis. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Foundations in Painting
description: The focus of this class is on perception and color, students learn to see the empirical world and respond to it by inventing a two- dimensional equivalent with oil paint.  Exercises and assignments focus on color use, composition and content, tactile paint surfaces, and basic material instruction.  This course is designed to prepare students for more advanced painting experiences by introducing the fundamental elements of the discipline of painting.

title: Painting the Human Form
description: This course focuses on the figure in painting, both formally and as a thematic topic. The first half of the semester introduces the processes of indirect painting developed by the Early Netherlandish Painters in 15th century europe. Students work from a single pose with a live model on one painting for half of the semester. We begin by making and priming our own cradled panels and then slowly work from underpainting through glazing and scumbling and finishing details. This project is contextualized by readings to help understand the interesting and problematic histories of classicism and realism. The second half of the semester focuses on a project in which the strategies and techniques from the first half are applied to more self-directed content.

title: Hybrid Painting as Contemporary Practice
description: Hybridization in the visual arts - sampling, appropriation, and the combination of different subjects, methods, and materials - is a widely used and accepted approach to image making.  Painters today borrow imagery, forms and styles from art history, quote directly from other artists, and popular culture, and use materials and techniques from non-art contexts.   This course introduces the historically complex role of the painter as one cultural sampler. Through a variety of projects, students will experiment and expand their painting practice using research and subject matter that reflect human and global concerns.  Critiques will concentrate on the balance between subject matter and aesthetic form to reinforce fundamental visual principles while encouraging exploration and investigation of personal language.

title: Painting Majors Studio. [anticipate 10 juniors]
description: Students develop productive studio habits, explore personal themes and begin to define their own visual language. They are encouraged to investigate a variety of materials and processes. Trips to artist studios, major museums and galleries supplement the educational experience. Critiques include discussions, lectures and demonstrations, and a variety of visiting artists are held regularly. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Painting Majors Studio. [anticipate 8 seniors]
description: This course provides an uninterrupted opportunity for the advanced painter to develop independently. Instructors critique, lecture, give demonstrations, and participate with students in both one-on-one situations and group discussions. Emphasis is placed on developing a personal painting process, while building and sustaining an extended body of work. The course also addresses the realities of being a working artist in contemporary culture (i.e. gallery representation, grants & funding, job information, etc.). Visiting artists, trips to museums and galleries, readings and lectures help to facilitate these goals. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Analogue Photography
description: Students are introduced to the vocabulary of photography through a series of projects designed to increase their perceptual skills. Emphasis is on exploring the world with a camera in order to understand the unique properties of photographic form and then to learn how to use these properties for communication and self-expression. Exposure, film, print development and analogue darkroom skills are the basis for this class.

title: Medium Format Photography
description: This course, based on a series of weekly assignments and critiques, leads the student through a systematic and comprehensive investigation of the elements of the photographic language.  Issues of the landscape, portrait, the constructed image, for example, are explored.  Emphasis is also placed on expanding and refining one's technical control of the medium along with developing the ability to discuss and critique each other's work.

title: Photography Majors Studio. [anticipate 4 juniors]
description: This is primarily a critique class in which each student can concentrate on several different self-directed projects. Group critiques are supplemented by discussions of articles about issues in the contemporary photography/art world. The course is designed to encourage each student to explore his/her individual photographic concerns. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Photography Majors Studio. [anticipate 4 seniors]
description: This is primarily a critique class in which each student can concentrate on a semester-long self-directed project. Group critiques are supplemented by discussions of articles about issues in the contemporary photography/art world. The course is designed to encourage each student to explore his/her individual photographic concerns developing a Thesis Presentation during the senior year. Major standing: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Intro to Printmaking Techniques
description: This studio course is an introduction to a variety of printmaking processes including: wood and linoleum carving, collagraphs, etching and monotypes. Students will learn and utilize new ways of creating imagery unique to printmaking, and gain expertise and control over a range of tools and materials with an emphasis on developing their skill through elements of design and conceptual ideation. Printing single and multiple plates and blocks in black and white and color will help students produce both consistent and variable editions as well as monoprints. Students will study the visual and material characteristics of the medium, incorporating their personal ideas and aesthetic. Hands-on in class work time will be supplemented with related presentations and demonstrations. Students will gain familiarity with safe, sustainable and professional printmaking practices and develop formal, material and verbal languages that will enhance any studio practice and critiquing skills. The course culminates in an editioned print portfolio exchange.

title: Intaglio Printmaking I
description: This studio course is an introduction to a variety of intaglio printmaking techniques including drypoint, hardground and softground etching, and aquatint. Students will learn editioning and monoprinting methods while developing their grasp over elements of design, conveying concepts, and capitalizing on materiality. Students will gain familiarity with safe and professional printmaking practices and develop a language for printmaking critique. Once basic methods of getting marks below the surface of copper plates are familiar, a range of image-expanding options such as chine collé (printing while collaging), and color printing processes are covered. This is a great studio class to explore your existing ideas related to drawing, painting and illustration.

title: Printmaking Majors Studio  [anticipate 2 juniors]
description: This course emphasizes the development and enhancement of each student’s studio practice and the production of a range of work in a range of media. The primary focus is on your work, looking specifically at how you articulate your ideas, the quality and quantity of your work and your contextualization of what you make. Directed assignments leave plenty of room for the assertion of self and guide students through generative and iterative processes designed to encourage experimentation, risk taking, and the deployment of already established passions as well as movement into unknown territories. Student work is refined through a rigorous critique process and individual studio meetings with faculty. Moving through a range of projects, students build skills and acquire the knowledge to critically position their work within the larger historical and conceptual framework that defines the increasingly broad field of print. Studio assignments, technical demonstrations, readings, archival research, presentations, critique, field trips, workshops and special projects all present students with new ideas and ways of making studio work and crafting a sustainable practice, while also helping students increase their communication and critical thinking skills. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Printmaking Majors Studio  [anticipate 5 seniors]
description: This course emphasizes the development and enhancement of your studio practice and the production of an increasingly cohesive body of work. The primary focus is on your work, looking specifically at how you articulate your ideas, the quality and quantity of your work and your contextualization of what you make. Seniors generally work in a self-directed manner, with any assignments or departmental projects leaving plenty of room for the assertion of self and guiding students through generative and iterative processes designed to foster the movement toward a cohesive thesis in the subsequent semester. Student work is refined through a rigorous critique process and individual studio meetings with faculty. Moving through a range of projects, students build skills and acquire the knowledge to critically position their work within the larger historical and conceptual framework that defines the increasingly broad field of print. Studio assignments, technical demonstrations, readings, archival research, presentations, critique, field trips, workshops and special projects all present students with new ideas and ways of making studio work and crafting a sustainable practice, while also helping students increase their communication and critical thinking skills. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: (PE)(S) Sculpture in Context: Installation, Multiples, Land Art
description: This course teaches the basic techniques while creating multiples & editions, installations, and land art. We will investigate the materials, methods, and concepts that serve as the foundation for creating three-dimensional artworks. Through weekly assignments, lectures, demonstrations, and discussions we will explore traditional and experimental approaches to the theory and practice involved in making sculpture. In each assignment, we will study the historical, cultural, and contemporary applications and implications of these given sculptural techniques. However, the subject matter for all assignments is open to your interpretation. Students will learn how to identify materials, and proper material usage, learn tools and safety within the Sculpture area, create finished works, and learn articulation of these materials (both physically and audibly) via critiques of finished works. The primary objective of this course is to develop the skills needed to interpret and articulate contemporary modes of creating sculpture that students will encounter as professional artists and creative problem solvers.

title: Mold Making: Form & Transformation
description: – 25 – STUDIO & SEMINAR COURSES   [continued] SEMINARS

title: Sculpture: Junior Major Studio. [anticipate 6 juniors]
description: This course emphasizes the making of sculpture: inspiration, identifying material resources, developing technical skills, craftsmanship and critical awareness of visual and structural integrity. Students will complete three works and supporting materials in the form of three-dimensional studies, experiments and drawings are expected. Students choose materials and subjective references, and works may incorporate resources and inspiration from many other disciplines. Students develop productive work habits and learn to take charge of their direction and keep to a schedule. Technical assistance is available to help with new processes. Students work in all three studios of the department. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Sculpture: Senior Major Studio (3-6 cr option) [anticipate 9 seniors]
description: This course equips students with the wherewithal to sustain artistic growth and emphasizes an independent and significant investigation of personal artistic interests and sensibilities. Students determine the nature of the work they produce, choosing technical means, materials, scale and context. Sculpture is approached as the perceptual and philosophical exploration of form and our interactions with it. Visual organization and craftsmanship, essential to making clear content, are stressed. Four complete works and supporting material in the form of three dimensional studies or experiments and drawings are expected, culminating in the presentation of a written thesis. Students work in all studios of the department. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Introduction to Textiles
description: This course provides an overview of introductory textile materials and fiber processes as well as concepts of textiles science and textile testing. This course combines lectures, labs, and hands-on studio study in a comprehensive overview of textiles as materials and their inherent properties, strengths, and weaknesses. Learning progresses from fiber to finished textiles throughout the semester as students learn to create yarn from raw fiber, create woven and felted textiles, and use both synthetic and natural dyes. Students learn to apply textile techniques to individual projects including choosing appropriate materials and structures and employing professional finishing and installation techniques for their textile works.

title: Introduction to Fashion and Apparel
description: The fundamentals of sewing and garment construction are developed as a foundation for beginning concept-driven apparel design in this course. Students learn to use industrial sewing equipment while creating technical samples for garment construction and finishing. In further study, students complete projects that begin to explore the expressive properties of design details, color, texture and materials, and introductory strategies for communicating ideas through garment design. The translation from 2D to 3D forms is investigated through construction and simple draping projects that investigate shape, silhouette, and scale of works in relation to the body.

title: (AE) Textile Printing
description: Working both traditionally and digitally, students explore concepts of motif and repeat as means to create printed patterns on textiles. Hands-on study will focus on technical means for producing print and pattern on cloth using screen printing with dye and pigment as well as basic direct printing methods. Students will learn to sketch and develop printed pattern ideas digitally and by hand with consideration to the inherent strengths and weaknesses of various repeat structures and types of patterns. Through projects and samples students build knowledge of various textile printing methods and practice designing and printing repeating patterns on cloth. Work expands in scale throughout the semester leading to large scale printed works and full size screen-printed repeats on cloth.

title: (AE) Advanced Pattern Drafting & Construction [anticipate 2 juniors]
description: Building upon foundation garments studied in TF 321, students further study flat pattern drafting and learn to develop unique garment styles in response to basic concepts. Students draft patterns for bifurcated garments, develop woven tops with closures and plackets, and study basic cut-and-sew knit patterning and construction. Prototypes are created for each style, allowing further study of garment construction, finishing, and fit assessment. As a capstone to the course, students complete a product development unit that includes market research, product development, prototyping, pricing, and developing professional technical sketches and specifications for production. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Advanced Projects in Textiles or Fashion [anticipate 8 seniors]
description: Seniors work through independent projects to develop advanced technical skill and conceptual rhetoric in their area of study in preparation for their thesis. Students develop independent projects in textiles or fashion design and create their own calendars to organize their semester goals. Example projects might include topics in advanced pattern drafting, advanced textile printing, advanced weaving, or other independent projects that expand upon previous courses the student has completed. Students develop independent design skills such as seeking resources, sourcing materials, time management, studio organization, and managing complex projects. Group critiques and studio visits provide feedback on both the technical and conceptual aspects of work. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: CURViture
description: This course examines the conceptual and structural possibilities of bending wood as it applies to functional and sculptural approaches. Assignments and exercises focus on the practice and practical application of solid wood and plywood. Exercises will cover simple and complex bends, fixture mechanics, and clamp/vacuum bag applications. Various bending methods will include brick-laying, lamination and steam. CNC technology may also be used to augment these techniques. Students will apply these newly acquired skills to make a minimum of one project.

title: Woodworking in Two Dimensions
description: This course centers around a variety of woodworking techniques that are either directly two-dimensional in nature or designed to augment traditionally two-dimensional mediums. Students will discover new methods of image making as well as techniques to enhance already existing studio practices, such as painting, illustration, photography or graphic design. Through multiple projects the primary topics covered in this studio course will be pyrography, marquetry & parquetry, framing and custom panel fabrication. All techniques will be explored through both analog and digital means. Pyrography, literally “writing with fire” will be experienced through both handheld woodburners and the Fab Lab laser etcher. Marquetry and Parquetry is the creation of images and patterns using primarily wood veneer and will be experienced manually with knives, fretsaws, and scrollsaws as well as digitally with the Fab Lab laser cutter. Students will learn a variety of different techniques relating to designing and fabricating frames from both hardwood and composite materials. Techniques and possibilities will be discussed and demonstrated that incorporate both traditional woodworking techniques and digital CNC routers. With no pre-requisites this course can also serve as an introduction to the 3rd floor Wood Studios and various modes of woodworking not typically captured within our Introductory Woodworking course. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week. No prerequisite.

title: Wood & Furniture Majors Studio  [anticipate 5 juniors]
description: This course explores the making of furniture & furniture related objects. Through technical demonstrations, conceptual discussions and material explorations, students will gather the skills needed to begin constructing their ideas with wood as their primary medium. Drawings and models will be used to generate and refine projects. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: Wood & Furniture Majors Studio  [anticipate 5 seniors]
description: Within this course, it is expected that each student begins to develop a significant body of work and the ability to articulate that work in preparation for their thesis and final semester. Multiple advanced techniques will be covered based on student designs, needs and trajectory. Students are required to organize, exhibit and document their work within an all-department exhibition at the end of this course. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 6 hours/week.

title: (PE) Public Engagement Minor Capstone
description: The Public Engagement Capstone with community-engaged component is a culminating experience for seniors in the minor. Students are encouraged to work independently, working with their chosen community partner prior to the capstone to develop a proposal. The faculty advisor, community partner and student meet prior, during and after the capstone project. The capstone is project based with a text component. Requirements of the text are determined with the faculty advisor in relationship to the work. Requirements for the capstone include a corollary statement of purpose, project plan, and studio component. Additionally, each capstone is shared publicly and  includes documentation that is permanently stored in the PE archive and MECA&D library. Students work with a faculty advisor under an independent study arrangement. Students can complete the Capstone requirement any time after their junior year, including summer.

title: (AE) Art & Entrepreneurship Minor Capstone
description: This course is a culminating experience for seniors in the Art & Entrepreneurship minor. Students will work with the minor coordinator to develop the concept and scope of the capstone project.

title: First Year Seminar
description: The First Year Seminar engages students in both co-curricular and curricular experiences. A weekly “assembly'' where first year students come together for workshops, collaborations and talks from community partners, guest artists and faculty. Designed to support a first year student's transition into college, the seminar builds community, introduces students to the College’s support services and establishes the foundation for being a successful art and design student.  Weekly 50 minute meeting, required of all first- year students.

title: 3YR Seminar: Fine Art Topics-Practice.  [anticipate 22 students over 2 sections]
3YR Seminar: Fine Art Topics-Practice.  [anticipate 22 students over 2 sections]

Professional Studio - Animation & Game Art. [anticipate 21 students]
description: Professional Studio provides students with time and space in which to focus on three core components of their education: academic skills, employability skills, and technical skills. This one-semester course is designed to deliver guidance and standards for entering into creative professions available with a BFA degree. Topics focus on essential skills required to plan, strategize, navigate, maintain, and grow as a creative person in a chosen career–to unite core skill components in their application towards achievable outcomes, functioning in a workplace, and initiating routine activities universal for any individual career path. These include professional writing and communications, applications and creative grants, taxes and finances, contracts, copyright, freelancing, and public speaking. Projects include a professional identity package, website, and business cards. Major requirement: 3 credits/semester; 2.5 hours/week.
