title: 19th-Century Media Worlds
description: This course explores some of the nineteenth-century media that shaped the art and visual culture of global modernity and, in numerous cases, that of other disciplinary spheres. Whether newly invented, ‘revived,’ or of longer standing, media including wood engravings, electrotypes, daguerreotypes, plaster casts, chromolithographs, woodblock prints, glass, and embroidery participated in local and global circuits of exchange, changing how people looked at and understood the world and themselves. Students will learn about diverse mediums’ material dimensions, movements, and legacies, as well as their adopters’ and users’ negotiations of invention and tradition.

title: Special Topics: Intro to Game Development
description: This course will introduce students to the foundational aspects of game development.  Starting with elemental lessons that teach students the basics of working in game engines and give insights about the game industry on a whole, we’ll move into design- or theory-based projects about game mechanics and art production. Game development is a complex, multidisciplinary practice, and this course will serve as the bedrock for the rest of your gamedev journey here at MECA&D.

title: Animation I
description: This course introduces and explores the twelve principles of animation as developed at the Disney Animation Studios. Assigned projects guide students through the technical and creative processes involved in 2D digital animation. Students apply the principles to create sequential drawings that give personality and emotion to moving objects and simple characters.

title: Ceramics Throwing- Beginning
description: Wheel throwing is the primary means of making ceramic forms in this course. Introductory and intermediate throwing skills, material concepts, glazing, and firing are covered. Reduction kiln firing concepts are introduced as the students construct functional and nonfunctional forms in clay. Students also gain familiarity with artists who use wheel processes to explore contemporary issues and design. The Raku process will also be covered.

title: Millennium Approaches: America in the 21st Century
description: *Requirement for all Sophomores. Course not available for Junior or Senior enrollment. The class is organized around five phrases that have become part of contemporary popular discourse, and each phrase is tied to key moments or events in 21st Century America. Using a diverse range of media—from political speeches to film and from memoir to music videos—and indexing key humanities concepts, the class traces the “reverb” of the event, helping students understand the historical context of contemporary conditions. Our key moments are five: The 2025 Executive Orders; the 2008 Obama election; the George Floyd uprising of 2020; the – 3 – ACADEMIC STUDIES   [continued] establishment of Guantanamo Bay in a post-9/11 world; and the anti-immigration rhetoric of 2016. Taking these as our points of departure we turn back and trace these moments through slavery, European migration into the United States, Japanese internment, the AIDS crisis, as well as immigration and the southern border. Requirement for all Sophomores: 3 credits/semester; 3 hours/week.

title: Introduction to Drawing
description: This course introduces drawing from perception, including techniques for measurement, contour, massing, volume, and rendering illumination through value. Compositional skills such as placement and figure/ground interaction are stressed.  Drawing materials include vine charcoal, compressed charcoal, stamp, and india ink; subject matter includes still life, interior, landscape, and the figure.

title: Digital Imaging: Drawing & Painting
description: This section explores the fundamentals of digital photography. Emphasis is placed on developing a proficiency with Adobe Photoshop, cameras, and printing in the pursuit of Foundation design and composition objectives.

title: 3D Materiality:  Textiles. week 1-7
description: This course investigates three-dimensional phenomena and cultivates an understanding of design and composition in three dimensions. This course equips students with the ability to create and discuss three-dimensional work with intention and precision. Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: 3D Materiality: Woodworking.  week 8-15
description: This course investigates three-dimensional phenomena and cultivates an understanding of design and composition in three dimensions. This course equips students with the ability to create and discuss three-dimensional work with intention and precision. Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: 3D Materiality:  Woodworking. week 1-7
description: This course investigates three-dimensional phenomena and cultivates an understanding of design and composition in three dimensions. This course equips students with the ability to create and discuss three-dimensional work with intention and precision. Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: 3D Materiality:  Textiles.  week 8-15
description: This course investigates three-dimensional phenomena and cultivates an understanding of design and composition in three dimensions. This course equips students with the ability to create and discuss three-dimensional work with intention and precision. Through the choice of paired 7-week mini courses, students will gain an applied understanding of the principles and elements of Three-Dimensional Design while acquiring an understanding of specific materials and fabrication techniques. Choice of paired study from the following departments: Ceramics, Metals, Sculpture, Textiles, or Woodworking. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: Two-Dimensional Design
description: This course cultivates an understanding of design and composition through an in-depth investigation of two-dimensional phenomena and perceptual color. Through the successful completion of carefully sequenced exercises, students will gain an understanding of composition and design while developing the dexterity and skills with digital tools necessary to create work with intention and precision. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: Two-Dimensional Design
description: This course cultivates an understanding of design and composition through an in-depth investigation of two-dimensional phenomena and perceptual color. Through the successful completion of carefully sequenced exercises, students will gain an understanding of composition and design while developing the dexterity and skills with digital tools necessary to create work with intention and precision. The course works in conjunction with all Foundation studio, Liberal Arts and Art History courses.

title: Perpetual Play
description: This introductory course explores the foundational tools, skills, and creative possibilities of graphic design. With a focus on both effective communication and personal expression, students will utilize typography, illustration, and playful experimentation to develop dynamic graphic systems. Student projects have included custom lettering, music cover art, and redesigning a game. Blending analog ideation with digital workflows, the curriculum features projects that bridge practical application with artistic expression. Coursework ranges from rapid, hands-on workshops to comprehensive, in-depth design projects.

title: Portrait Drawing in Illustration: People and Animals
description: This course is a beginning-level portraiture class and is devoted to the study of portrait work as it pertains to the field of illustration. Assignments will be based on portrait work in a range of styles from realistic and detailed to stripped-down and minimalist. Human forms will be studied extensively (and traditionally) within the first few weeks. Live models and photo references will be the basis for much of this study. Animal forms will be introduced in a similar fashion. Assignments will range from in-class studies to homework involving the execution of finished work with the portrait featured in an illustrative context.

title: Setting the Scene
description: Backgrounds should never be an afterthought or considered filler. Care and consideration in creating backgrounds should be as important as the characters who dwell in them. This class teaches the fundamentals of constructing spaces, environments, and settings, in themselves and tailored for characters." World-build with props, vehicles, architecture, and natural forms on par with character work. Focus on perspective drawing including linear one, two-point, and isometric, as well as exposure to alternative approaches, and constructive drawing will be used as systems to manipulate observed, referenced, and imagined sources alike, shoring up sound 2-D design principles to create effective compositions for illustrative ends.

title: Foundations in Painting
description: The focus of this class is on perception and color, students learn to see the empirical world and respond to it by inventing a two- dimensional equivalent with oil paint.  Exercises and assignments focus on color use, composition and content, tactile paint surfaces, and basic material instruction.  This course is designed to prepare students for more advanced painting experiences by introducing the fundamental elements of the discipline of painting.

title: Experimental Photography
description: This course will explore the malleable and plural ways artists engage with the photographic medium. The assignments and coursework will encourage students to incorporate an interdisciplinary approach to the medium. We will cover present and past artists who have used photography as an extension of their studio practice and have had an experimental approach to the medium. Students will be expected to think outside the box and utilize new methods for creating photographic work. Lectures, group discussions, and assignments will cover photography, silkscreen, collages, artist books, and sculptural considerations.

title: Introduction to Woodworking
description: This is a beginning level introduction to wood as a visual and building material. Assignments and exercises focus on the application of various techniques such as carving and fabrication to create dynamic forms that explore function & composition. The use of power and hand tools will be applied to create a variety of objects that illustrate an in-depth understanding of working with wood. Students will first design and create custom croquet mallets, utilizing largely reductive methods, which will be used in an outdoor croquet match. A small Shaker style end table will be made as an instructional exercise leading into a final custom occasional table design and build.

title: English Composition
description: Reading and writing are essential parts of college learning and successful art & design practice. In this class, students will explore a variety of approaches to reading and writing, including close reading and textual analysis, critical and comparative analysis, and reflective, argumentative writing. Students will be introduced to three major genres of text: the novel; the graphic novel; and poetry.
